summary: DHH analyzes a blog post by a skeptical developer named Paul who dismisses Ruby on Rails as hype, arguing that Paul's resistance stems from psychological 'survival rules' — defensive heuristics formed after being burned by past technology choices. Rather than attacking Paul directly, DHH frames the skepticism as a natural defense mechanism that any disruptive technology must face. He argues that when people invoke survival rules, they either reexamine them or construct dichotomies to dismiss the challenge. DHH sees this resistance as a positive sign that Rails is forcing developers to confront their assumptions, and points to Rails' open, readable source code as the key to helping skeptics make more informed decisions.
key_points: Developers build psychological 'survival rules' after being burned by adopting technologies that later failed, and these rules operate unconsciously until provoked

When confronted with enthusiasm that violates their survival rules, people either reexamine the rule or construct a dichotomy to dismiss the challenge — Paul chose the latter by dividing developers into serious professionals vs. hobbyists
DHH reframes skepticism toward Rails not as a Rails-specific problem but as a universal defense mechanism any new technology faces
The ability to read, modify, and run Rails source code lowers the barrier to informed evaluation, similar to how 'View Source' helped the web take off
Forcing survival rules to be invoked and examined is itself a sign of progress for a new technology
money_quotes: We deal with those scars by building survival rules like 'don't trust something until it's been around for years (or you will be burned again)'.
Survival rules are rarely something we think of consciously — unless provoked.
This is a fundamental defense mechanism that any new technology, development approach, and way of thinking will encounter.
I consider it a sign of good things when we force survival rules to be invoked (and subsequently examined). That's definitely the first step.
The second step is of course to figure out how to help people make more informed decisions, so they have better grounds on which to decide whether the survival rule is helping or hurting them.
In much the same way that the web took off because of 'View Source', Rails is taking off because it lowers the barrier to entry and holds nothing back.
key_insight: Resistance to new technology is rarely about the technology itself — it's a psychological defense mechanism born from past failures, and the real challenge is helping people reexamine their survival rules rather than dismiss them.
